ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03301103 represents green tea beverage as a category; no specific canned/RTD green tea code exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers green tea as a beverage category; no specific RTD canned variant code exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 16313001 refers to green tea as a substance; used as closest parent for this canned RTD product.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard canned serving of green tea is approximately 300 ml (300 g), equivalent to a single commercially sold can as commonly served in Singapore food establishments.

nutrient_source.title: Beverages, tea, green, ready-to-drink, unsweetened (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, NDB No. 14355 and related entries); supplemented with USDA FoodData Central entry for ready-to-drink green tea
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for brewed green tea (unsweetened, per 100g/100ml). Canned RTD green tea sold in Singapore may contain added sugar; values here represent a lightly sweetened commercial RTD green tea profile consistent with USDA Branded Food data for comparable products (e.g., approximately 4–5 g sugar per 100 ml). Where a specific brand is unknown, the conservative lightly sweetened profile is used. Core macros reflect this adapted estimate.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 (Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC); supplemented by University of Sydney GI Database convention for non-glycemic beverages
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: Plain and lightly sweetened green tea contains negligible glycemic carbohydrates. No direct GI measurement exists for canned green tea in the University of Sydney GI Database. By convention for non-glycemic beverages (plain tea, water), glycemic_index is set to 1 (effectively zero) and gi_category is 'low'. glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0 given the negligible carbohydrate content per serving. Reference: Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (approximately 2 mg per 100 ml)
hypertension.guidance: Canned green tea is very low in sodium (~6 mg per 300 ml can) and is generally safe for individuals with hypertension; however, patients should verify the sodium content of the specific brand, as some commercial RTD teas may contain added sodium or flavourings.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible glycemic carbohydrate load; polyphenol content may confer modest insulin-sensitising benefit
type2_diabetes.guidance: Lightly sweetened canned green tea has a very low glycemic load and is generally suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; patients should choose unsweetened or low-sugar variants and check the nutrition label for added sugar content, as heavily sweetened RTD teas can contribute meaningfully to daily carbohydrate intake.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Zero saturated fat, zero cholesterol, and presence of catechins (green tea polyphenols) with potential cardioprotective effects
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Canned green tea contains no saturated fat or cholesterol and provides catechins that have been associated with modest reductions in LDL cholesterol and blood pressure in epidemiological studies; individuals with cardiovascular disease may include unsweetened or lightly sweetened green tea as part of a heart-healthy diet.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Canned green tea (绿茶罐装) is a ready-to-drink (RTD) beverage made from brewed green tea leaves, water, and typically a small amount of sugar or sweetener, commercially packaged in a sealed aluminium can. It is widely consumed across East and Southeast Asia, including Singapore, where it is commonly available at hawker centres, restaurants, and convenience stores. The beverage has a light, slightly grassy, mildly astringent flavour with a subtle sweetness in commercially sweetened variants. Green tea is rich in catechins — particularly epigallocatechin gallate (EGCG) — which are polyphenolic antioxidants associated with anti-inflammatory, cardioprotective, and potential metabolic benefits. Nutritionally, canned green tea is very low in calories (approximately 4 kcal per 100 ml for lightly sweetened versions), contains negligible protein and fat, and has a very low glycemic load, making it suitable for most dietary patterns including those managing Type 2 Diabetes and cardiovascular disease. Sodium content is minimal. The beverage is naturally caffeine-containing, which should be noted for individuals sensitive to caffeine. It is suitable for vegetarian, vegan, halal, and gluten-free diets. In Singapore, canned green tea is served as a standalone beverage accompaniment to Chinese, Cantonese, and roasted meat dishes.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a can of green tea?
Is green tea good for diabetes?
Is RTD green tea suitable for hypertension?
What is the sugar content of canned green tea?
What are the benefits of green tea catechins?
Can diabetics drink canned green tea?
meal_context: beverage accompaniment to roasted duck or roasted meat dishes

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for brewed green tea and USDA Branded Food data for comparable RTD green tea products. The specific brand served at Wan Jia Fu Chinese Restaurant is unknown, so exact sugar and additive content cannot be confirmed. Values represent a conservative lightly sweetened RTD green tea profile. Confidence is medium rather than high due to brand-level uncertainty.
notes: 1. protein_g is naturally zero as green tea is a plant-derived aqueous beverage containing no protein in detectable quantities per USDA FoodData Central. 2. fat_g is naturally zero; green tea contains no lipids per USDA FoodData Central. 3. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, and cholesterol_mg are confirmed zero for plain and lightly sweetened green tea per USDA FoodData Central. 4. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero for liquid tea beverages per USDA FoodData Central (fibre is not present in the liquid extract). 5. glycemic_index set to 1 (effectively zero) as this beverage contains negligible glycemic carbohydrates; gi_category low per convention for non-glycemic beverages. glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0. 6. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central source for brewed green tea. 7. vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source database for this beverage. 8. vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source database. 9. vitamin_b3_niacin_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source database. 10. vitamin_b6_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source database. 11. vitamin_b12_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the source database. 12. folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in the source database. 13. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the source database. 14. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source database. 15. phosphorus_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source database for this beverage. 16. magnesium_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source database. 17. zinc_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source database. 18. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the source database. 19. The specific brand of canned green tea served at Wan Jia Fu Chinese Restaurant (万家福创新中国菜) is not confirmed; nutrient values represent a lightly sweetened RTD green tea profile. 20. Multilingual names follow Singaporean dish rules (English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) as this is a Chinese-cuisine item served in Singapore. 21. FoodOn, FoodEx2, and SNOMED CT codes use closest_parent match type as no exact RTD canned green tea code exists in these ontologies.
